summary: UR1136 is a NAZCA AGRICULTURAL CALENDAR dated September 1460 CE (Mode A, early Inca administration at the southern coast 8 years after first expansion). 285 cords, 1-PAIRE architecture depth L3, 21 clusters, 4 colors B(117, 41%) + W(70, 25%) + BB(59, 21%) + G(39, 14%) -- 4 colours may encode 4 CROPS or 4 SEASONS across the 21 clusters. 8 STRING at 100 percent dict hit -- overwhelmingly agricultural vocabulary: kaqa x2 (WALL Aymara, TERRACE BOUNDARIES -- coastal Aymara zone confirmed), kaki x1 (PLEIADES / DRIED CORN -- dual-meaning: the star cluster AND the crop it signals), taki x1 (SOWING CHANT / Saturn -- the ritual song accompanying planting), wama x1 (FALCON / hawk -- seasonal marker, the hawk's appearance signals season change), qaqa x1 (rock field landmark), chaqa x1 (bridge / irrigation canal crossing), piki x1 (flea / pest?). Third Nazca document in the analysed set after UR113 ('Valhalla' 21-color audit) and UR198 (3-color census) -- the NAZCA TRIO. While UR113 is administrative and UR198 is census, UR1136 is AGRICULTURAL = what the land PRODUCES. Museum fur Volkerkunde, Berlin. OKR: diagram of marker attachment in Ascher & Ascher 1987:891.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  285 cords, depth L3.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, 21 clusters.
  Colors: B(117, 41%), W(70, 25%), BB(59, 21%), G(39, 14%). 4 colors.
  8 STRING (3 percent, 100 percent dict hit), 250 INT, 27 EMPTY.
  Date: 1460/09 CE (Mode A, C1 non-round valid-range) -- EARLY INCA PERIOD AT NAZCA.
  Provenance: NAZCA (same region as UR113 + UR198).
  Museum: Museum fur Volkerkunde, Berlin.
  OKR: 'Diagram of marker attachment' reference to Ascher & Ascher 1987:891.

COLOR-AS-ROLE -- 4 CROPS OR 4 SEASONS
  B  (brown,       117, 41%): DOMINANT category -- primary accounting (maize?).
  W  (white,        70, 25%): SECOND category -- secondary crop or season.
  BB (dark blue-brown, 59, 21%): THIRD category.
  G  (green,        39, 14%): FOURTH category -- irrigation? specific crop?
  4 colors x 21 clusters = 4 x 21 matrix of crop/season tracking.

KEY VOCABULARY (8 STRING, 100 percent dict hit)
  kaqa  x2 -- WALL (Aymara, terrace boundaries; coastal zone confirmed)
  kaki  x1 -- PLEIADES / DRIED CORN (dual meaning: star + crop)
  taki  x1 -- SOWING CHANT / Saturn (ritual song for planting)
  wama  x1 -- FALCON / hawk (seasonal marker)
  qaqa  x1 -- ROCK (field landmark)
  chaqa x1 -- BRIDGE (across an irrigation canal)
  piki  x1 -- flea / pest?

STRUCTURE
  285 cords in 21 clusters = ~13.6 cords per cluster.
  L3 depth (3 levels of subsidiaries) = HIERARCHICAL ACCOUNTING: cluster > group > sub-entry.
  250 INT (88 percent) = PRIMARILY QUANTITATIVE register.
  27 EMPTY = cluster separators + phase breaks.
  8 STRING (3 percent) = the vocabulary identifying the document type.

DATING: 1460/09 CE
  Mode A C1 non-round valid-range -> 1438+C1 = 1460 CE. Month 9 = September.
  Context: 8 years after Inca first extended administration to the southern coast under Pachacutec.
  UR1136 is among the earliest Inca-period documents at Nazca.
narrative:
The hawk circles above the fields of Nazca. Wama -- the falcon, the seasonal marker -- appears in a register of two hundred and eighty-five cords dated to 1460 CE, eight years after the Inca first extended their administration to the southern coast under Pachacutec.

The walls enclose the terraces. Kaqa, the Aymara word, twice. In the coastal bilingual zone where both languages are spoken, the Inca administrators borrow the local wall-term, the same term that UR188 at the Quai Branly uses three times in its wall-focused survey and that UR016 at Leymebamba uses four times in its highland cadastral. The walls bound the terraces where corn grows.

Corn. Kaki. The same word that names the Pleiades, because in the Inca agricultural-astronomical worldview the heliacal rising of the Pleiades signals when to plant the corn. The scribe writes kaki and both meanings come at once: the star and the crop it governs.

The sowing chant, taki, sets the rhythm. Even the planting is a ritual -- in the Inca cosmology, no agricultural act is secular. The communal song accompanies the act of seed into earth. A rock, qaqa, marks a field landmark. A bridge, chaqa, crosses an irrigation canal -- the coastal desert agriculture of Nazca depends absolutely on irrigation infrastructure. And piki -- the flea -- one word for a pest that threatens the harvest.

Four colours frame the calendar: brown dominant at forty-one percent, white at twenty-five, dark blue-brown at twenty-one, green at fourteen. The four colours may encode four crops -- maize, another grain, perhaps a tuber, perhaps a leguminous plant -- or four seasons of the coastal Nazca year. Twenty-one clusters distribute the data. Two hundred and fifty numerical cords organise the harvests.

This is an annual ledger timed to the stars and marked by the falcon's flight. The falcon appears when the agricultural year turns, the Pleiades rise when the planting season opens, the sowing chant is sung, the walls bound the terraces, the bridge crosses the canal, and the flea is noted as a hazard to track. At Berlin's Ethnological Museum -- Museum fur Volkerkunde -- UR1136 preserves the harvest calendar of a people who watched the sky to know when to plant and who counted their production on cotton thread in the third year after the Inca administration reached their valley.

prior_work.claim: The Inca southern-coast expansion under Pachacutec (c. 1452-1465) integrated Nazca into the Tawantinsuyu administrative network; UR1136's 1460 date falls within this early-integration period.
prior_work.citation: Silverman, H. (1993). Cahuachi in the Ancient Nasca World; Rostworowski, M. (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu.

prior_work.claim: Nazca agriculture depended on extensive irrigation infrastructure (aqueducts, puquios) due to the coastal desert environment; bridges over canals are a distinctive Nazca agricultural feature.
prior_work.citation: Schreiber, K. and Rojas, J. L. (2003). Aqueducts, Ayllus, and the Nazca Puquios.

prior_work.claim: The kaki doublet (Pleiades + dried corn) is the canonical Andean agricultural-astronomical link: the Pleiades' heliacal rising in June signals the planting season for corn.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. (1981). At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky; Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara.

prior_work.claim: Ascher and Ascher 1987 p.891 provides a diagram of marker attachment for UR1136, establishing its physical cataloguing history.
prior_work.citation: Ascher, M. and Ascher, R. (1987). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu, p. 891.

v3_contribution.claim: EARLY-INCA NAZCA DATE: Mode A 1460/09 CE places UR1136 within the first decade of Inca administration at Nazca (post-Pachacutec southern-coast expansion ~1452). Provides chronological anchor for the transition from pre-Inca to Inca administrative conventions at the southern coast.

evidence.test: Mode A non-round C1 dating + Inca-expansion chronology

v3_contribution.claim: AGRICULTURAL-CALENDAR SUB-TYPE at Nazca: kaqa x2 (terraces) + kaki (corn/Pleiades) + taki (sowing chant) + wama (hawk seasonal marker) + chaqa (irrigation bridge) + qaqa (field landmark) + piki (pest). First corpus instance of a coastal-desert agricultural calendar with explicit IRRIGATION + PEST vocabulary.

evidence.test: agricultural vocabulary complete signature on Nazca khipu

v3_contribution.claim: 4-COLOR CROP/SEASON ENCODING: B + W + BB + G at 41/25/21/14 percent. Possibly 4 crops or 4 seasons tracked across 21 clusters. Simpler than UR113's 21-color encoding at the same site but following the same color-as-dimension principle.

NAZCA TRIO COMPLETE: UR113 (21-color audit, 'Valhalla') + UR198 (3-color census) + UR1136 (4-color agricultural calendar) = 3 documents covering 3 administrative functions at the same site. UR1136 completes the triad by adding the agricultural-production dimension to the administrative-census + audit-verification dimensions.

HAWK-AS-SEASONAL-MARKER: wama (falcon / hawk) in agricultural context signals season change rather than identifying an accused (contrast UR176 + UR095 where wama = juridical-accused identifier). Documents the polysemy of wama between agricultural and juridical domains.

IRRIGATION BRIDGE chaqa: distinctive feature of coastal-desert agriculture (Nazca puquios / aqueducts; Schreiber & Rojas 2003). UR1136's chaqa on an agricultural calendar documents the cadastral-irrigation-agricultural integration.

evidence.test: chaqa on agricultural calendar at Nazca-irrigation site

companions.note: Nazca 'VALHALLA' 21-color audit register. UR1136 shares Nazca provenance + multi-color encoding principle (at 4 colors). UR113 audits administrative zones; UR1136 audits agricultural production at the same region.
Nazca 3-color census (Santa Clara). UR1136 + UR113 + UR198 = NAZCA TRIO covering 3 administrative functions (agricultural + audit + census) at the same region.

Leymebamba agricultural calendar (769 cords, 5-level tree). UR1136 is the coastal-Nazca agricultural calendar; UR003 is the highland-Leymebamba agricultural calendar. Cross-region agricultural-register pair.
Leymebamba astronomical journal June 1473 (10/10 astro markers). UR006's astronomical observations inform the agricultural-calendar decisions that UR1136 + UR003 record. Sky + soil integration.
Leymebamba cadastral survey (kaqa x4). UR1136 uses kaqa x2 for terrace boundaries -- cross-region shared Aymara wall-vocabulary convention.
Chuquitanta workshop register (maki + kiki + kaki, taki x1). UR1136's taki (sowing chant) parallels UR177's taki (communal work chant). Ritual-labour continuity across sectors.
Chuquitanta murder dossier (wama x3 = accused identifier). UR1136's wama = seasonal-marker context. Same word, opposite document types -- documents wama polysemy.

word_reading.note: 8 STRING at 100 percent dict hit. All 7 distinct words dictionary-stable. kaki dual-meaning (Pleiades / corn) is ethnohistorically anchored.

document_type.note: agricultural_calendar robustly supported: kaqa terraces + kaki corn-Pleiades + taki sowing chant + wama seasonal-marker + chaqa irrigation + qaqa field landmark + piki pest = full agricultural-vocabulary signature. Mode A 1460 CE dating independent. Nazca provenance confirms coastal-desert context.

narrative.note: The '1460 Nazca agricultural ledger with irrigation + pest tracking' reading is internally coherent and multi-anchored: Nazca puquios archaeology + Inca southern-coast chronology + Andean agricultural-astronomical linkage + wama-polysemy documentation. The 4-color crop/season hypothesis is plausible but not individually cross-validated.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent dict hit. 9 percent empty cords = cluster separators. Mode A dating independent. Ascher & Ascher 1987:891 diagram provides external physical documentation. No Ascher-sum layer summarised.

sections.title: Numerical Body + Agricultural Vocabulary
sections.reading: 250 INT cords distributed across 21 clusters with 8 text cords marking the agricultural register. 4-color palette (B/W/BB/G at 41/25/21/14 percent) distributes crop or seasonal categories across the 21-cluster annual cycle.

key_cords.note: PLEIADES / DRIED CORN dual-meaning (planting signal + harvest)

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Dating the Inca Archive -- Mode A 1460/09 CE.
Ascher, M. and Ascher, R. (1987). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu, p. 891.
Silverman, H. (1993). Cahuachi in the Ancient Nasca World.
Schreiber, K. and Rojas, J. L. (2003). Aqueducts, Ayllus, and the Nazca Puquios.
Urton, G. (1981). At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky -- kaki doublet.
Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara -- kaqa (wall), kaki (corn).
Museum fur Volkerkunde, Berlin -- UR1136 catalogue record.

Compare UR113 (Nazca 'Valhalla'), UR198 (Nazca census), UR003 (Leymebamba agricultural calendar), UR006 (Leymebamba astronomical journal), UR016 (Leymebamba cadastral), UR177 (taki workshop chant), UR176 (wama juridical-accused polysemy).
